attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for her first antenatal visit at 12 weeks gestation. This is her second pregnancy; she has one healthy child. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins. During confidential screening, she discloses consuming three to four standard drinks of vodka most evenings since becoming pregnant. She appears anxious about potential harm to her baby.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her body mass index is 24 kg/m2. Obstetric examination is consistent with dates.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most significant risk to fetal development from continued alcohol exposure?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with significant alcohol consumption during pregnancy, particularly concerning given the critical period of organogenesis during the first trimester. Alcohol readily crosses the placenta and can affect multiple organ systems, but the developing central nervous system is particularly vulnerable.

Neurodevelopmental disorders are the most significant and permanent effects of prenatal alcohol exposure. These include intellectual disability, behavioural problems, poor executive function, and learning difficulties. These effects can occur even with moderate alcohol consumption and may not be apparent until later in childhood.

Neural tube defects are primarily associated with folate deficiency rather than alcohol exposure. While alcohol can interfere with folate metabolism, neural tube defects are not a primary feature of fetal alcohol spectrum disorder (FASD).

Cardiac malformations, while possible with alcohol exposure, are not the most common or significant feature of FASD. When present, they typically occur as part of a constellation of other developmental abnormalities.

Skeletal dysplasia is not a characteristic feature of FASD. While growth restriction and some minor skeletal abnormalities can occur, true skeletal dysplasia is not typically associated with alcohol exposure.

Renal agenesis is not a primary feature of FASD. While alcohol can affect kidney development, complete renal agenesis is not a characteristic finding.

The management of this patient should focus on immediate cessation of alcohol consumption, referral to appropriate support services, and close monitoring of fetal growth and development. There is no known safe level of alcohol consumption during pregnancy, and the effects can be permanent and devastating.
learningPoints: Fetal alcohol spectrum disorder can occur with any amount of alcohol exposure during pregnancy, with no known safe level.
Regular alcohol consumption during pregnancy increases risk of miscarriage, stillbirth, and growth restriction.
First trimester alcohol exposure particularly affects organ development and facial features.
references: Australian Institute of Family Studies. Fetal alcohol spectrum disorder (FASD): an update on policy and practice in Australia [Internet]. Canberra (AU): AIFS; 2022 [cited 2024 Dec 10]. Available from: https://aifs.gov.au/resources/policy-and-practice-papers/fetal-alcohol-spectrum-disorder-fasd-update-policy-and

Feldman HS, Jones KL, Lindsay S, Slymen D, Klonoff-Cohen H, Kao K, Rao S, Chambers C. Prenatal alcohol exposure patterns and alcohol-related birth defects and growth deficiencies: a prospective study. Alcohol Clin Exp Res. 2012 Apr;36(4):670-6. doi: 10.1111/j.1530-0277.2011.01664.x. Epub 2012 Jan 17. PMID: 22250768.
